---
title: "When in the year do feeder cattle fetch the most?"
subtitle: "Weekly seasonality · Clovis, NM auction"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Caveat before scrolling.** Seasonality is a pattern, not a prediction. Any single year can look very different from the weekly average shown here — drought, calf-crop timing, regional demand, and futures-market moves all shift the intra-year curve. See [Methodology](methodology/index.qmd) for how the window was chosen.
:::

## About this page

The companion to the [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd). That page answers *"what does a heavier calf bring?"*; this page answers *"when in the year does a given-weight calf bring the most?"* The chart shows all six 100-lb bins from 300 lb to 899 lb: **click a weight class in the legend to hide it; double-click to isolate it.** Three bins are emphasized in solid colors (400–499, 600–699, 700–799) — they bracket the typical marketing decision (calf-on-the-cow, background/grass-season exit, feedlot-ready) — and the other three are rendered in lighter accents so the chart reads cleanly when all six are visible. Two button rows above the chart let you pick a **year window** — All, Last 5 years, Last 3 years, or the latest calendar year — in either **nominal dollars** (top row) or **real December 2025 dollars** (bottom row). Only one button is highlighted at a time across both rows; clicking any button sets the chart to that combination.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: data-load

# Load via the shared Clovis loader — see comment in price-weight.qmd's
# data-load chunk for the union+dedupe semantics.

from pathlib import Path
import sys
import pandas as pd

PROCESSED = Path("..") / "data" / "processed"
CPI_PATH = PROCESSED / "cpi_latest.parquet"

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path("..").resolve()))
from pipelines.clovis.load import load_clovis_combined  # noqa: E402

data_is_placeholder = False
data_source_label = None

_loaded = load_clovis_combined(PROCESSED)
if _loaded is not None:
    df = _loaded.df
    data_source_label = _loaded.data_source_label
else:
    # Synthetic stand-in. Clearly labelled so it is never mistaken for real data.
    import numpy as np
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=2026)
    # 52 weeks × 3 bins × 2 classes × 6 years = seed rows with a gentle seasonal shape
    rows = []
    bins = {"400-499 lb": 270, "600-699 lb": 230, "700-799 lb": 200}
    for year in range(2020, 2026):
        for week in range(1, 53):
            for cl in ("Steers", "Heifers"):
                for wb, base in bins.items():
                    seasonal = 15 * (1 - abs(week - 30) / 25)  # peak near week 30
                    noise = rng.normal(0, 5)
                    mean = base + seasonal + noise - (12 if cl == "Heifers" else 0) + (year - 2020) * 6
                    rows.append({
                        "auction_date": pd.Timestamp(f"{year}-01-01") + pd.Timedelta(weeks=week-1),
                        "weight_bin": wb,
                        "class": cl,
                        "head_count": int(rng.integers(20, 200)),
                        "price_avg": round(mean, 2),
                    })
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    # Add frame/muscle columns so the downstream filter accepts these rows.
    df["frame"] = "Medium and Large"
    df["muscle_grade"] = "1"
    df["weight_break_low"] = df["weight_bin"].str[:3].astype(int)
    df["avg_weight"] = df["weight_break_low"] + 50
    data_is_placeholder = True
    data_source_label = "synthetic stand-in (no live data available)"

if data_is_placeholder:
    print("SYNTHETIC STAND-IN DATA — for layout only. Not real prices.")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: data-shape

# Same filtering as the price-weight page: Medium and Large, muscle grade 1,
# 100-lb bins derived from weight_break_low (fall back to avg_weight for
# rows before MARS added that field, pre-2024). All six 100-lb bins from
# 300 lb to 899 lb are kept; the legend lets the reader hide or isolate
# any combination.

import pandas as pd

if not data_is_placeholder:
    slim = df[
        (df["class"].isin(["Steers", "Heifers"]))
        & (df["frame"] == "Medium and Large")
        & (df["muscle_grade"] == "1")
    ].copy()
    wb = slim["weight_break_low"]
    aw = slim["avg_weight"]
    raw_weight = wb.fillna(aw)
    slim = slim[raw_weight.notna()].copy()
    slim["bin_lo"] = ((raw_weight[raw_weight.notna()] // 100) * 100).astype(int)
    slim = slim[(slim["bin_lo"] >= 300) & (slim["bin_lo"] <= 800)]
    slim["bin_hi"] = slim["bin_lo"] + 99
    slim["weight_bin"] = slim.apply(
        lambda r: f"{int(r['bin_lo'])}–{int(r['bin_hi'])} lb", axis=1
    )
    work = slim[
        ["auction_date", "weight_bin", "class",
         "price_low", "price_avg", "price_high"]
    ].copy()
else:
    work = df.copy()
    work["price_low"] = work["price_avg"]  # placeholder has only price_avg
    work["price_high"] = work["price_avg"]

# Keep all six 100-lb bins the pipeline produces; the reader picks which to
# view via the legend. Three of the six are emphasized in solid colors
# (400–499, 600–699, 700–799) — they bracket the typical marketing decision —
# and the other three are rendered in lighter accents so the chart reads
# cleanly when all six are visible.
SEASONAL_BINS = [
    "300–399 lb", "400–499 lb", "500–599 lb",
    "600–699 lb", "700–799 lb", "800–899 lb",
]
work = work[work["weight_bin"].isin(SEASONAL_BINS)].copy()

# Window metadata for the caption
date_min = pd.to_datetime(work["auction_date"]).min()
date_max = pd.to_datetime(work["auction_date"]).max()
n_years = pd.to_datetime(work["auction_date"]).dt.year.nunique()
n_obs = len(work)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: deflate-to-dec-2025

# Identical deflation logic to the price-weight page: CPI-U NSA, Dec 2025 basis,
# per-month join. See site/price-weight.qmd for the full explanatory comment.
import sys as _sys
import pandas as pd

BASIS_PERIOD = pd.Timestamp("2025-12-01")
cpi_basis_label = "December 2025"

deflation_available = False
if CPI_PATH.exists():
    cpi = pd.read_parquet(CPI_PATH)[["period", "cpi_u"]].copy()
    cpi["period"] = pd.to_datetime(cpi["period"])
    basis_rows = cpi[cpi["period"] == BASIS_PERIOD]
    if not basis_rows.empty:
        cpi_basis_value = float(basis_rows["cpi_u"].iloc[0])
        deflation_available = True
    else:
        fallback = cpi.sort_values("period").iloc[-1]
        cpi_basis_value = float(fallback["cpi_u"])
        cpi_basis_label = pd.to_datetime(fallback["period"]).strftime("%B %Y")
        deflation_available = True

if deflation_available:
    work["period_month"] = work["auction_date"].dt.to_period("M").dt.to_timestamp()
    cpi_lookup = cpi.set_index("period")["cpi_u"]
    work["cpi_u_month"] = work["period_month"].map(cpi_lookup)
    work["deflator"] = cpi_basis_value / work["cpi_u_month"]
    missing_cpi = int(work["deflator"].isna().sum())
    if missing_cpi:
        print(
            f"[deflate] {missing_cpi} observations lack a CPI month-match; "
            "leaving nominal.", file=_sys.stderr,
        )
    work["deflator"] = work["deflator"].fillna(1.0)
    work["price_avg_real"] = work["price_avg"] * work["deflator"]
else:
    work["price_avg_real"] = work["price_avg"]
```

::: {.callout-important}
**Data source**: `{python} data_source_label` ·
**Window**: `{python} date_min.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')` to `{python} date_max.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')` (`{python} n_years` calendar years) ·
**Observations**: `{python} f"{n_obs:,}"` across three bins ·
**Grade shown**: Medium and Large, muscle grade 1 ·
**Deflation basis**: `{python} cpi_basis_label` CPI-U NSA (`CUUR0000SA0`).
:::

## Weekly price pattern

Each line is the average price for a weight class at each week of the calendar year, averaged across every year in the selected window. The top panel shows Steers; the bottom shows Heifers. Two button rows above the panels let the reader pick the **year window** (All, Last 5 years, Last 3 years, or the latest calendar year) and the **dollar basis** (nominal or real Dec 2025); each click sets a new view, defaulting the other dimension to its baseline. Hover any point to see the exact value and the number of observations behind it.

```{python}
#| echo: false

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Aggregate weekly mean for each (class, bin) series. ISO-week 1-53; most
# years have 52, a few have 53. We collapse week 53 into week 52 so the
# x-axis fits a clean 1-52 grid without an orphan bar.
work["iso_week"] = work["auction_date"].dt.isocalendar().week.astype(int)
work.loc[work["iso_week"] == 53, "iso_week"] = 52


def _weekly(work_df, price_col):
    agg = (
        work_df.groupby(["class", "weight_bin", "iso_week"], as_index=False)
               .agg(price=(price_col, "mean"), n=(price_col, "count"))
    )
    return agg


# CI diagnostic — logged to stderr, invisible on the rendered page.
import sys as _sys
print(
    f"[seasonality] work rows={len(work)}",
    file=_sys.stderr,
)

# ---------- year windows ----------
_ad = pd.to_datetime(work["auction_date"])
_anchor = _ad.max()
_anchor_year = int(_anchor.year)
YEAR_WINDOWS = [
    ("all",    "All",            _ad.min(),                                    _anchor),
    ("5y",     "Last 5 years",   _anchor - pd.DateOffset(years=5),             _anchor),
    ("3y",     "Last 3 years",   _anchor - pd.DateOffset(years=3),             _anchor),
    ("latest", f"{_anchor_year}", pd.Timestamp(_anchor_year, 1, 1),            _anchor),
]

# Six-class palette. Three lines emphasized in solid colors — they bracket
# the typical marketing decision (calf-on-the-cow / background-grass-season
# / feedlot-ready). The other three are kept on the chart in lighter
# accents for context.
COLOR_BY_BIN = {
    "300–399 lb": "#6a3d9a",
    "400–499 lb": "#000000",
    "500–599 lb": "#2ca02c",
    "600–699 lb": "#d17a00",
    "700–799 lb": "#1f77b4",
    "800–899 lb": "#8b0000",
}
BIN_ORDER = [
    "300–399 lb", "400–499 lb", "500–599 lb",
    "600–699 lb", "700–799 lb", "800–899 lb",
]

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True,
    vertical_spacing=0.10,
    subplot_titles=(
        "NM Clovis Steers — Cash Prices",
        "NM Clovis Heifers — Cash Prices",
    ),
)

# 4 year-windows × 2 modes = 8 view combinations × 12 traces per view (6
# bins × 2 classes) = 96 total traces. Each click on a year or mode button
# sets a new visibility mask. Switching one dimension resets the other to
# its baseline (year=All, mode=nominal).

def _add_class(agg_df, cls, row, visible, show_legend):
    """Add 6 bin-traces for one class. Always emits 6 traces (empties for
    bins with no data) so the visibility mask index stays predictable."""
    for wb in BIN_ORDER:
        sub = (
            agg_df[(agg_df["class"] == cls) & (agg_df["weight_bin"] == wb)]
            .sort_values("iso_week")
        )
        if sub.empty:
            fig.add_trace(
                go.Scatter(
                    x=[], y=[], name=f"{wb}",
                    legendgroup=wb, showlegend=False, visible=visible,
                ),
                row=row, col=1,
            )
            continue
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Scatter(
                x=sub["iso_week"].tolist(),
                y=sub["price"].tolist(),
                mode="lines+markers",
                name=wb,
                legendgroup=wb,
                showlegend=show_legend,
                visible=visible,
                line=dict(color=COLOR_BY_BIN[wb], width=2),
                marker=dict(color=COLOR_BY_BIN[wb], size=5),
                customdata=sub["n"].tolist(),
                hovertemplate=(
                    f"<b>{wb} · {cls}</b><br>"
                    "Week %{x}<br>"
                    "Avg price: $%{y:.2f}/cwt<br>"
                    "n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>"
                ),
            ),
            row=row, col=1,
        )


# Trace order:
#   For each year_window in YEAR_WINDOWS:
#       For each mode in (nominal, real):
#           Steers-bins-1..6 (legend on), Heifers-bins-1..6 (legend off)
# 12 traces per (year, mode) group, 8 groups, 96 traces total.
N_TRACES_PER_GROUP = 12
N_GROUPS = len(YEAR_WINDOWS) * 2
N_TRACES = N_GROUPS * N_TRACES_PER_GROUP

for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    sub = work[(_ad >= lo_d) & (_ad <= hi_d)]
    seas_n = _weekly(sub, "price_avg")
    seas_r = _weekly(sub, "price_avg_real")
    is_default = (y_idx == 0)  # year=All & mode=nominal initially visible
    # Legend entries only need to appear for the active group; we render
    # them on EVERY group's nominal Steers row. Plotly auto-hides legend
    # entries whose trace is visible=False, so the legend reflects the
    # active view automatically.
    _add_class(seas_n, "Steers",  row=1, visible=is_default, show_legend=True)
    _add_class(seas_n, "Heifers", row=2, visible=is_default, show_legend=False)
    _add_class(seas_r, "Steers",  row=1, visible=False,      show_legend=True)
    _add_class(seas_r, "Heifers", row=2, visible=False,      show_legend=False)


def _visibility_for(year_idx: int, mode_idx: int) -> list[bool]:
    out = [False] * N_TRACES
    group = year_idx * 2 + mode_idx
    start = group * N_TRACES_PER_GROUP
    for i in range(start, start + N_TRACES_PER_GROUP):
        out[i] = True
    return out


# ---------- two button rows, one per dollar basis ----------
# Each button is a complete (year, mode) selector. Clicking a button in
# Row 0 (nominal) ALSO deactivates Row 1's highlight via
# "updatemenus[1].active": -1, and vice versa — so at any moment, exactly
# one button across both rows is highlighted, and the chart and the
# highlight always agree.

NOMINAL_Y_TITLES = {
    "yaxis.title.text": "Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
    "yaxis2.title.text": "Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
}
REAL_Y_TITLES = {
    "yaxis.title.text": f"Avg price, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
    "yaxis2.title.text": f"Avg price, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
}

nominal_row_buttons = []
for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    nominal_row_buttons.append(dict(
        label=y_label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(y_idx, 0)},
            {
                **NOMINAL_Y_TITLES,
                "updatemenus[1].active": -1,
            },
        ],
    ))

real_row_buttons = []
for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    real_row_buttons.append(dict(
        label=y_label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(y_idx, 1)},
            {
                **REAL_Y_TITLES,
                "updatemenus[0].active": -1,
            },
        ],
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    height=900,
    # Top margin needs to accommodate two button rows + labels + the
    # six-entry horizontal legend below them. Buttons go at y=1.32 / 1.20,
    # legend at y=1.035, leaving ~0.165 paper of space between them.
    margin=dict(t=230, b=60, l=80, r=30),
    hovermode="x unified",
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    font=dict(family="Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif", size=13, color="#222"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.035, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
    annotations=[
        dict(text="<b>Nominal $</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.32, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
        dict(text=f"<b>Real ({cpi_basis_label} $)</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.20, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
    ],
    updatemenus=[
        dict(  # nominal row (index 0)
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.32,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=nominal_row_buttons,
        ),
        dict(  # real row (index 1)
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=-1,
            x=0.18, y=1.20,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=real_row_buttons,
        ),
    ],
)

# Axes. Print every week number 1-52 under the bottom panel — a small font
# keeps all 52 labels readable without collision. The explicit range clips
# Plotly's default ~5% padding so the first and last ticks sit at the
# panel edges rather than leaving an empty week -1 and week 53+ gutter.
fig.update_xaxes(
    tickmode="linear", tick0=1, dtick=1,
    tickfont=dict(size=9),
    showgrid=False, linecolor="#888", mirror=False, ticks="outside",
    range=[0.5, 52.5],
)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Week of calendar year", row=2, col=1)

fig.update_yaxes(
    showgrid=True, gridcolor="#e5e5e5",
    tickprefix="$", linecolor="#888", mirror=False, ticks="outside",
    title_text="Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
)

fig.show()
```

## How to read this

**Flat lines mean no seasonality.** If a weight class's line is close to horizontal across the 52-week calendar, that class shows no clear "best time of year" to sell — the price you'd expect depends on year-to-year market conditions more than on the calendar date. Feeder cattle at Clovis are mostly like this: the intra-year pattern is subtle relative to the year-to-year volatility.

**Sloped or peaked lines mean seasonality is worth naming.** Look for consistent humps — common across all three weight classes in the same weeks — rather than one noisy class's outlier. The price-weight spread between classes (vertical distance between the three lines) is what the [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd) zooms in on; this page is about the horizontal dimension.

**Why three weight classes are emphasized.** The 400–499, 600–699, and 700–799 lines bracket the typical marketing decision — below 500 lb is "calf on the cow" territory, 600–700 lb is the background/grass-season exit, and 700–800 lb is the feedlot-ready window. Those three are drawn in solid black, orange, and blue. The other three (300–399, 500–599, 800–899) are kept on the chart in lighter accents so the full range is available for any reader who wants it, without overwhelming the producer who's just trying to read the three decision-relevant lines. Click any legend entry to hide or isolate it; double-click to show *only* that class.

## See also

- [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd) — the complementary view, sliced by weight class instead of by week.
- [Methodology → Clovis pipeline](methodology/clovis.qmd) — every filter, bin, and edge case behind the snapshot this page reads from.
- [Methodology → CPI deflation](methodology/cpi.qmd) — how the December-2025-basis deflator is computed.

## Download

- [Parquet snapshot](../data/processed/clovis_latest.parquet) — Clovis feeder-cattle archive, latest MARS vintage
- [Vintage manifest](../data/processed/clovis_MANIFEST.json) — SHA-256 of every snapshot the pipeline has committed
- [CPI deflator](../data/processed/cpi_latest.parquet) — BLS CPI-U NSA (`CUUR0000SA0`), the deflation basis

## What's next on this page

- [ ] Year-range slider matching the price-weight explorer (Phase 2).
- [ ] Percentile bands (10/50/90) around each line for the per-week dispersion.
- [ ] Side-by-side "this year vs. historical average" overlay for the current marketing season.
